title: Moduli Spaces of Codimension-One Subspaces in a Linear Variety and Their Tropicalization
overall_summary: The paper studies, for a fixed linear subspace X of projective or affine space, the moduli space L(X) of codimension-one linear subspaces contained in X, embedded in the appropriate Grassmannian by Plücker coordinates. Its main algebraic result is that L(X) is itself a linear subspace of the Plücker ambient projective space, with two explicit descriptions: as the rowspace of a cofactor-type matrix V and as the kernel of an incidence matrix U. The paper then identifies the matroid of L(X) in several equivalent ways, most importantly as the matroid of lines of the coordinate hyperplane arrangement restricted to X, and in the generic case as a relabeled Dilworth truncation of the free matroid. After tropicalization, in the constant-coefficient case, trop(L(X)) is the Bergman fan of this matroid; in nonconstant coefficients there is a valuated analogue. For the original problem, the most relevant contribution is a precise combinatorial model for the codimension-one part of the space of tropicalized linear subspaces inside a fixed tropical linear space, together with examples showing that tropical point-hyperplane self-duality is subtle and not automatic.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be an algebraically closed field. Let X ⊂ K^n be a (d+1)-dimensional linear subspace, let W ∈ K^{(d+1)×n} be a matrix whose rowspace is X, and let L(X) ⊂ Gr(d,n) ⊂ P^{binom(n,d)-1} be the set of d-dimensional linear subspaces Y ⊂ X, embedded by their Plücker coordinates indexed by d-subsets J ⊂ [n]. For i ∈ {1,...,d+1} and J ∈ binom([n],d), let W^J_{−i} be the d×d submatrix of W using columns J and all rows except row i, and define the (d+1)×binom(n,d) matrix V by V_{i,J} = (−1)^{i+1} det(W^J_{−i}). Then L(X) is equal to the projectivization of the rowspace of V. In particular L(X) is a d-dimensional projective linear subspace of P^{binom(n,d)-1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Every d-dimensional subspace Y of X can be written as rowspace(TW), where T is a d×(d+1) matrix of rank d. For a d-subset J ⊂ [n], the Plücker coordinate det((TW)^J) is computed by Cauchy-Binet as the sum over i=1,...,d+1 of det(T^{[d+1]∖i}) det(W^J_{−i}), where T^{[d+1]∖i} is T with column i removed. Setting t_i = (−1)^{i+1} det(T^{[d+1]∖i}), the whole Plücker vector of Y is tV. Conversely, as T ranges over full-rank d×(d+1) matrices, its maximal minors range over Gr(d,d+1) ≅ P^d, so the possible vectors t fill the projective rowspace of V. To prove that this rowspace has projective dimension d, choose a (d+1)-subset I ⊂ [n] such that W^I is invertible. The square submatrix of V with columns I∖{j}, j ∈ I, is, up to alternating signs, the cofactor matrix of W^I; since W^I is invertible, this cofactor matrix is invertible. Hence rank(V)=d+1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With K, X, W and L(X) as in Theorem 1, write q_I = det(W^I) for each (d+1)-subset I ⊂ [n]. Let U be the matrix whose rows are indexed by pairs (A,B) with A ∈ binom([n],d−1) and B ∈ binom([n],d+2), whose columns are indexed by J ∈ binom([n],d), and whose entry is U_{(A,B),J} = (−1)^{|[i]∩A|+|[i]∩B|} q_{B∖i} if J=A∪{i} for some i ∈ B∖A, and 0 otherwise. Equivalently, the row (A,B) records the coefficients of the incidence relation I_{A,B} = sum_{i∈B∖A} (−1)^{|[i]∩A|+|[i]∩B|} q_{B∖i} P_{A∪i}. Then L(X), as a projective subspace of P^{binom(n,d)-1}, is equal to the projectivized kernel of U.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The incidence relations I_{A,B}=0 are the classical linear equations expressing that a d-dimensional subspace with Plücker coordinates P_J is contained in the fixed (d+1)-dimensional space X with Plücker coordinates q_I. Therefore ker(U) ∩ Gr(d,n) is exactly L(X). Theorem 1 already gives L(X) as a projective d-plane, so it remains to see that ker(U) has projective dimension at most d. Choose C ∈ binom([n],d+1) with q_C ≠ 0. For each d-subset I not contained in C, choose i ∈ I∖C and consider the row indexed by A=I∖i and B=C∪i. In column I, this row has coefficient ±q_C. The possible other nonzero entries in this row occur in columns whose intersection size with C is ordered strictly on one side of |I∩C|; ordering the d-subsets not contained in C accordingly gives a triangular submatrix with diagonal entries ±q_C. Thus U has at least binom(n,d)−(d+1) independent rows, so ker(U) has dimension at most d+1 as a vector space, i.e. projective dimension at most d. Since it contains L(X), equality follows.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S be the polynomial ring with variables q_I indexed by (d+1)-subsets I ⊂ [n] and variables P_J indexed by d-subsets J ⊂ [n]. For D ∈ binom([n],d−1) and E ∈ binom([n],d+2), define the codimension-one incidence relation I_{D,E} = sum_{i∈E∖D} (−1)^{|[i]∩D|+|[i]∩E|} q_{E∖i} P_{D∪i}, and let I be the ideal generated by all such I_{D,E}. For A ∈ binom([n],d−1) and B ∈ binom([n],d+1), let R_{A,B} = sum_{i∈B∖A} (−1)^{|[i]∩A|+|[i]∩B|} P_{A∪i} P_{B∖i} be the Plücker relation for Gr(d,n). Then for every fixed C ∈ binom([n],d+1), every Plücker relation R_{A,B} lies in the saturation (I : <q_C>^∞). Equivalently, for each R_{A,B} there exists N ≥ 0 such that q_C^N R_{A,B} ∈ I.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is an algebraic double induction built from three explicit sign-sensitive identities. First, Lemma 30 gives, for suitable A,B,C and an element a of A used to replace entries by elements of C, an identity of the form sum_i ± P_{B∖i} I_{A∖a∪i, C∪a} = R_{A,B} q_C + sum_j ± R_{A∖a∪j,B} q_{C∖j∪a}. The signs are the standard Plücker/incidence signs, and the key sign check is that the exponent attached to the second sum is independent of the summation index i; this is verified by a modulo-2 comparison of the order positions of a,i,j. Second, Lemma 31 proves the special case R_{A,C} ∈ (I:<q_C>^∞) by induction on |A∖C|: if A⊂C the relation is zero, and otherwise Lemma 30 with B=C expresses R_{A,C} q_C modulo I in terms of relations R_{A∖a∪j,C} with smaller |(A∖a∪j)∖C|. Third, Lemma 32 handles the case A⊂C while B varies: for b ∈ B∖C it gives an identity R_{A,B} q_C + sum_{j∈C∖B} ± R_{A,B∖b∪j} q_{C∪b∖j} = ± P_{B∖b} I_{A,C∪b} + sum_{i∈B∖A, i≠b} ± P_{A∪i} I_{B∖i∖b,C∪b}. The right-hand side is in I, and the relations in the left-hand sum have larger intersection with C. The theorem first proves the base case A⊂C by induction on |B∩C| using Lemma 31 and Lemma 32. It then proves the general case by induction on |A∩C|, using Lemma 30 to replace an element outside C by an element of C; the lower-order Plücker relations are already in the saturation, and the incidence terms are in I. Saturation by q_C allows the repeated appearances of the factor q_C to be cleared.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X ⊂ K^n be a (d+1)-dimensional linear subspace over an algebraically closed field K, let L(X) ⊂ Gr(d,n) ⊂ P^{binom(n,d)-1} be the projective space of d-dimensional subspaces contained in X, and let W be a (d+1)×n matrix whose rowspace is X. Let V be the matrix V_{i,J} = (−1)^{i+1} det(W^J_{−i}) from Theorem 1, and let U be the incidence matrix from Corollary 2. Let A(X) be the hyperplane arrangement in K^{d+1} with hyperplanes H_k = {x ∈ K^{d+1} : w_k · x = 0}, where w_k is the k-th column of W. For each d-subset J ⊂ [n], define ℓ_J = ∩_{k∈J} H_k if this intersection is one-dimensional and ℓ_J=0 otherwise. Then the rank-(d+1) matroid of the projective linear subspace L(X) has the following equivalent descriptions: (i) the matroid of dependencies among the columns of V; (ii) the dual matroid to the matroid of dependencies among the columns of U; and (iii) the matroid of lines of A(X), namely the matroid on ground set binom([n],d) whose independent sets are collections of the lines ℓ_J spanning a subspace of the corresponding dimension, with zero ℓ_J treated as loops.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Description (i) is immediate from Theorem 1: a projective linear subspace represented as the rowspace of V has associated matroid equal to the column matroid of V. Description (ii) follows from Corollary 2 and linear matroid duality: if a subspace is the kernel of U, then its representing matroid is dual to the column matroid of U. For (iii), note that ℓ_J is the left kernel of the (d+1)×d submatrix W^J. This intersection is a line exactly when W^J has rank d, equivalently when at least one maximal minor of W^J is nonzero. The maximal minors of W^J are exactly the entries of the column V_J, up to the signs in the definition of V. When W^J has rank d, Cramer's rule says that V_J spans the left kernel ℓ_J. Thus linear dependencies among the columns V_J are exactly linear dependencies among the corresponding lines ℓ_J, and zero columns correspond to the zero ℓ_J.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 7 and Remark 20 (valuated analogue)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume K carries a non-Archimedean valuation and X is a linear subspace not necessarily defined over a trivially valued subfield. The paper states that a valuated analogue of Theorem 6 holds with the same proof: the valuated matroid of the linear subspace L(X) is computed from the valuations of the maximal minors of the matrix V, equivalently as the dual valuated matroid obtained from U. Consequently trop(L(X)) is the tropical linear space encoded by this valuated matroid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: No separate proof is given in the paper. The point is that the linear-algebraic descriptions L(X)=rowspace(V) and L(X)=ker(U) remain valid over the valued field. A linear subspace over a valued field determines a valuated matroid by taking valuations of the maximal minors of a representing matrix, and passing from a kernel description to a rowspace description corresponds to valuated matroid duality. Thus the same matrices V and U that represent the ordinary matroid in constant coefficients represent the valuated matroid in nonconstant coefficients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For fixed d and n, there is a Zariski open dense subset of the Grassmannian Gr(d+1,n) such that for every (d+1)-dimensional linear subspace X in this subset, the matroid of L(X) is the same. The paper calls such subspaces X generic. It also warns that this generic subset can be strictly smaller than the set of X whose own matroid is the uniform matroid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Work on the affine chart of Gr(d+1,n) where q_{[d+1]} ≠ 0 and normalize q_{[d+1]}=1. Row-reduce W so that its first d+1 columns form the identity matrix. In this gauge, the entries of V can be written directly in terms of the Plücker coordinates q_I of X: V_{i,J} = (−1)^{|[i]∩J|} q_{J∪{i}} if i∉J, and V_{i,J}=0 if i∈J. Therefore every maximal minor of V is a polynomial in the Plücker coordinates q_I, restricted to the Grassmannian. Some of these polynomials may vanish identically on Gr(d+1,n), while the others cut out proper closed subsets because the Grassmannian is irreducible. Requiring all non-identically-zero maximal minors of V to be nonzero defines a Zariski open dense subset on which exactly the same subsets of columns of V are bases. Hence the matroid of L(X) is constant on this subset.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 13 (Brylawski, quoted)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a rank-e matroid on n elements realized by a configuration P of n points in P^{e−1}. For 1 ≤ k ≤ e, let D_k(M) denote the k-th Dilworth truncation: its ground set is the set of rank-k flats of M, and a set {J_1,...,J_m} of such flats is independent if for every nonempty subset S ⊂ {1,...,m}, the rank in M of the union of the corresponding flats is at least |S|+k−1. Let H ⊂ P^{e−1} be a generic projective subspace of codimension k−1. Each rank-k flat F spanned by P, of projective dimension k−1, intersects H in a point h_F. Then the point configuration {h_F} in H realizes the Dilworth truncation D_k(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this theorem from Brylawski and does not prove it. It is used as a geometric coordinatization of Dilworth truncation: intersect all k-dimensional spans of the original point configuration with one generic complementary subspace, and the resulting points realize precisely the combinatorial truncation D_k(M).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X ∈ Gr(d+1,n) be generic in the sense of Proposition 9. Let U_{n,n} be the free matroid on [n]. For k=n−d, let eD_k(U_{n,n}) be the relabeling of the Dilworth truncation D_k(U_{n,n}) by taking complements: its ground set is binom([n],d), and a collection of d-subsets J_1,...,J_m is independent exactly when, for every nonempty subcollection of size s, the intersection of the corresponding J_l has size at most d+1−s. Then the matroid of L(X) is eD_{n−d}(U_{n,n}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: View X as a generic projective d-plane in P^{n−1}, hence of codimension n−d−1. The n coordinate points e_i in P^{n−1} realize the free matroid U_{n,n}. For each subset J ⊂ [n] of size n−d, let F_J be the projective span of {e_i : i∈J}; equivalently F_J is the intersection of the coordinate hyperplanes H_i={x_i=0} for i∉J. Since X is generic, X∩F_J is a point h_J. By Brylawski's Theorem 13 with k=n−d, the points h_J realize D_{n−d}(U_{n,n}). On the other hand, h_J = X ∩ (∩_{i∉J} H_i) corresponds exactly to the line ℓ_{J^c} in the restricted coordinate hyperplane arrangement A(X) appearing in Theorem 6. Therefore the matroid of L(X), which is the matroid of these lines, is obtained from D_{n−d}(U_{n,n}) by relabeling J by its complement J^c, i.e. it is eD_{n−d}(U_{n,n}).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 18 (Ardila-Klivans and Feichtner-Sturmfels, quoted)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a linear subspace over a valued field but defined over a subfield on which the valuation is trivial, and let A be a matrix whose rowspace is X. Let M be the matroid of the columns of A. For u ∈ R^n, let F(u) be the flag of subsets of [n] obtained by grouping the coordinates of u by equal values and ordering the groups by increasing coordinate value. A flag is a flag of flats of M if every subset appearing in the flag is a flat of M. Then the tropicalization trop(X) is the Bergman fan of M, namely the union of the weight cones of all flags of flats of M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this standard theorem and uses it as the bridge from linear spaces to matroids. The defining tropical equations of a constant-coefficient linear space require that, on every circuit of the matroid, the maximum of the corresponding coordinates be attained at least twice. Ardila-Klivans and Feichtner-Sturmfels identify exactly the weight vectors satisfying these circuit conditions with those whose coordinate flag is a flag of flats. Thus the tropicalization depends only on the underlying matroid and equals its Bergman fan.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 19
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X ⊂ P^{n−1} be a d-dimensional projective linear subspace defined over a trivially valued subfield, and let L(X) ⊂ Gr(d,n) ⊂ P^{binom(n,d)-1} be the locus of projective (d−1)-planes contained in X. Equivalently, the affine cone over X has vector dimension d+1. Then trop(L(X)) is the Bergman fan of each of the following equivalent matroids: (i) the column matroid of the matrix V with entries V_{i,J}= (−1)^{i+1} det(W^J_{−i}) for a matrix W whose rowspace is the affine cone over X; (ii) the dual of the column matroid of the incidence matrix U; and (iii) the matroid of lines of the coordinate hyperplane arrangement induced on X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 6 identifies the ordinary matroid of the constant-coefficient linear subspace L(X) in three equivalent ways. Since L(X) is itself a constant-coefficient linear subspace by Theorem 1, Theorem 18 applies directly: its tropicalization is the Bergman fan of its associated matroid. Substituting the three descriptions from Theorem 6 gives the three listed descriptions of trop(L(X)).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 21
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Over the complex numbers C, there is a Zariski open dense subset of Gr_C(d+1,n) such that for all constant-coefficient subspaces X in this subset, the Bergman fan trop(L(X)) is the same. This subset is exactly the generic subset of Proposition 9.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Proposition 9, the matroid of L(X) is constant on the generic open dense subset of Gr(d+1,n). In constant coefficients, Corollary 19 says trop(L(X)) is the Bergman fan of this matroid. Since a Bergman fan depends only on the matroid, trop(L(X)) is constant throughout that same generic subset.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 23
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X ∈ Gr(d+1,n) be generic in the sense of Proposition 9, and let trop(L(X)) be the tropicalization of the moduli space of d-dimensional linear subspaces contained in X. Then trop(L(X)), denoted in the paper by L_trop(trop(X)) in this generic situation, is the Bergman fan of the relabeled Dilworth truncation eD_{n−d}(U_{n,n}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 16 identifies the matroid of L(X) for generic X as eD_{n−d}(U_{n,n}). Applying Corollary 19 to the constant-coefficient linear subspace L(X) then identifies trop(L(X)) with the Bergman fan of that matroid.

proof_techniques.name: Cauchy-Binet cofactor parametrization of codimension-one subspaces
proof_techniques.description: The paper replaces the nonlinear condition Y ⊂ X, with Y a codimension-one subspace of X, by an explicit linear parametrization in Plücker space. If X is the rowspace of a (d+1)×n matrix W and Y is the rowspace of TW for a d×(d+1) matrix T, Cauchy-Binet expresses every d×d minor of TW as a linear combination of the d×d minors obtained from W by deleting one row. These minors are assembled into a cofactor-type matrix V, and the Plücker vector of Y is tV, where t is the vector of maximal minors of T. Since Gr(d,d+1) is projective space, t ranges over all of P^d, making L(X) a linear space.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a concrete model for the realizable codimension-one tropical linear subspaces inside a fixed tropicalized linear space. In attempts to construct or obstruct an order-reversing involution on a relative Dressian, the codimension-one layer can be studied through the explicit matrix V rather than through abstract containment alone.

proof_techniques.name: Incidence equations plus dimension forcing
proof_techniques.description: The containment Y ⊂ X is encoded by classical linear incidence relations in Plücker coordinates. The paper forms a matrix U of these relations and proves that its projective kernel equals L(X), not merely that its intersection with the Grassmannian equals L(X). The key extra step is a rank lower bound on U: after choosing a nonzero Plücker coordinate q_C of X, selected incidence rows form a triangular submatrix with diagonal entries ±q_C.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Relative Dressians are defined by tropical containment conditions. This incidence-matrix viewpoint suggests a way to isolate tropical hyperplanes inside a fixed tropical linear space by tropicalizing explicit linear incidence relations, and it gives rank or dimension constraints that may obstruct a proposed global duality.

proof_techniques.name: Saturation argument showing incidence implies Plücker in codimension one
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3 proves that, in the universal polynomial ring with both the Plücker coordinates q_I of X and the Plücker coordinates P_J of Y as variables, the codimension-one incidence ideal implies the Plücker ideal of Y after saturating by any chosen nonzero q_C. The proof uses explicit identities that express q_C times a Plücker relation as a combination of incidence relations plus Plücker relations closer to a chosen base case, then runs a double induction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is useful if one wants to tropicalize relative incidence conditions without separately imposing all Plücker conditions in codimension one. It suggests that codimension-one containment may have a simpler algebraic or valuated structure than arbitrary containment in the relative Dressian.

proof_techniques.name: Matroid of lines of a restricted coordinate arrangement
proof_techniques.description: The paper identifies the matroid of L(X) with the matroid of the lines obtained by intersecting d hyperplanes in the coordinate hyperplane arrangement restricted to X. The bridge is Cramer's rule: the column V_J is a spanning vector for the line ℓ_J = ∩_{j∈J} H_j whenever that intersection is one-dimensional, and it is zero otherwise. Thus dependencies among Plücker-coordinate columns are dependencies among arrangement lines.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing involution on the lattice of flats resembles a polarity or duality on incidence data. This theorem supplies a precise arrangement-theoretic object controlling hyperplanes inside a tropical linear space, so one can test whether the flat involution is compatible with the matroid-of-lines construction.

proof_techniques.name: Generic constancy via Plücker-minor nonvanishing
proof_techniques.description: After choosing a Plücker chart q_[d+1]=1 and row-reducing W, the entries of V become either zero or signed Plücker coordinates q_{J∪i} of X. Therefore the bases of the matroid of L(X) are controlled by maximal minors of V, which are regular functions on Gr(d+1,n). Requiring every non-identically-zero such minor to be nonzero gives a single open dense locus where the matroid of L(X) is constant.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If studying a relative Dressian by strata of matroids or valuated matroids, this technique identifies the generic codimension-one behavior and separates it from special degenerations. A proposed involution can be checked first on this generic model before addressing nongeneric strata.

proof_techniques.name: Dilworth truncation as the generic hyperplane-moduli matroid
proof_techniques.description: For generic X, the matroid of L(X) is described without coordinates: it is the relabeled Dilworth truncation eD_{n−d}(U_{n,n}). The proof uses Brylawski's realization of Dilworth truncation by intersecting all k-flats spanned by a point configuration with a generic complementary subspace. In the coordinate setting, these intersection points correspond, after complement relabeling, to the lines of the restricted coordinate hyperplane arrangement.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Dilworth truncations provide explicit combinatorial invariants of the codimension-one part of the relative tropical geometry. These invariants may be compared with the flat lattice involution in the original problem, especially for Boolean or uniform examples where the flat involution is explicit.

proof_techniques.name: Tropicalization through Bergman fans and valuated matroids
proof_techniques.description: Once L(X) is known to be a linear space, its tropicalization is controlled entirely by its matroid in constant coefficients, or by its valuated matroid in nonconstant coefficients. Thus the paper converts a moduli problem about tropicalized subspaces into the computation of a matroid or valuated matroid from V or U. In constant coefficients, trop(L(X)) is the Bergman fan of the matroid of lines of A(X).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The relative Dressian is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. Bergman fan descriptions translate parts of this order into matroidal data, which can be used to test whether an order-reversing map could preserve dimensions, ranks, flats, or incidence relations.

proof_techniques.name: Using examples to detect failure of naive tropical self-duality
proof_techniques.description: The paper analyzes explicit tropical planes and their spaces of tropicalized lines. For the standard tropical plane, the generic space of tropicalized lines is the Bergman fan of eD_2(U_{4,4}), whose coarse link is the Petersen graph, rather than an evident copy of the original plane. For nongeneric planes, the matroid of lines may fail to be simple and may fail to be a Dilworth truncation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: These examples are useful test cases for any proposed extension of a flat-lattice involution to a relative Dressian. They show that classical point-hyperplane duality does not automatically tropicalize to a self-duality of the whole moduli space of subspaces.

other_useful_info: The paper distinguishes carefully between the space of tropicalized codimension-one subspaces contained in trop(X) and the larger space of all tropical linear subspaces contained in trop(X); the former is a realizability-sensitive tropicalized Fano scheme, while the original problem's relative Dressian may include non-realizable tropical subspaces. The introduction explicitly notes that self-duality of tropical planes is not automatic: for example, the space of tropical lines in the standard tropical plane in R^3 cannot naturally be identified with the plane itself. Example 15 describes eD_{n−2}(U_{n,n}) concretely: its ground set is the set of 2-subsets of [n], its 3-circuits are exactly triples {a,a_1},{a,a_2},{a,a_3} sharing a common element, its 4-circuits are the 4-element sets containing no such 3-circuit, and it has no larger circuits; it is represented by pairwise intersections of n generic lines in P^2. Example 22, following Lamboglia, shows that two different algebraic lifts X_1 and X_2 with the same tropicalization can have different trop(L(X_i)); in the Gr(3,6) example one lift is generic while another satisfies the extra equation q124 q356 − q123 q456 = 0. Example 25 computes the generic space of tropicalized lines in the Bergman fan of U_{3,4}: it is the Bergman fan of eD_2(U_{4,4}), with fine link the Petersen graph plus three extra vertices and coarse link the Petersen graph. Example 26 shows that for a nongeneric plane, the matroid of L(X) is still the matroid of lines of A(X) but need not be simple and need not be a Dilworth truncation. Related papers cited by the authors that may be relevant are Brylawski [Bry85] on Dilworth truncations, Ardila-Billey [AB07] on generic flag arrangements, Lamboglia [Lam18] on tropical Fano schemes, and Brandt-Eur-Zhang [BEZ21] on tropical flag varieties and flag Dressians.
why_relevant: Studies the moduli space of codimension-one linear subspaces inside a fixed linear variety and its tropicalization. Its matroid-of-lines/adjoint constructions are directly useful for understanding hyperplanes inside Trop(M) and potential duality operations.
